front: Essay: this 1971 essay argues that artistic achievement is determined by social, cultural, and psychological factors rather than inherent genius
back: "Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?" (Linda Nochlin)
work: "Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?" (1971)

front: Essay: this essay concludes by extensively discussing Rosa Bonheur and The Horse Fair, arguing that institutional support is necessary for achievement
back: "Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?" (Linda Nochlin)
work: "Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?" (1971)

front: Essay: this essay cites Johann Zoffany's group portrait of the Royal Academy, where Angelica Kauffman and Mary Moser appear only as paintings on the wall due to the ban on women studying nude models
back: "Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?" (Linda Nochlin)
work: "Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?" (1971)

front: Essay: this essay includes a section titled 'The Question of the Nude'

work: "Why Have There Been No Great Women Artists?" (1971)

front: Essay: this essay's author points out that many great artists throughout history had fathers who were artists (e.g. Picasso)

front: Essay: this essay uses the example of Cimabue discovering Giotto as a shepherd boy to illustrate the myth of innate genius

front: Essay: a follow-up to this essay, titled 'Thirty Years After,' examines the continuing impact of artists like Louise Bourgeois

front: Essay: this essay analyzes Jean-Leon Gerome's The Snake Charmer, describing it as having an 'absence of history' and an 'insistent, sexually charged mystery'
Essay: this essay describes ten 'resolutely alienated' men watching with 'childish, trancelike concentration' while the audience is given a 'beguiling rear view'
Essay: this essay also discusses Delacroix's The Death of Sardanapalus as an example of using the East to portray sadistic sexual desires
Book: this monograph on 19th-century art opens by examining Courbet's The Meeting as 'A Portrait of the Artist as a Wandering Jew'
Book: Courbet's The Wounded Man provides the cover for this art historian's monograph on Courbet
Book: this 2006 book examines how the 'visceral eye' views bathers and discusses the 'death of the heroic landscape'
Art Historian: this thinker argued against the Hockney-Falco thesis by pointing out the realistic texture of wedding gowns and cloth
Art Historian: this thinker wrote about Edward Hopper's windows as tending to 'lock the static figures into place'
Art Historian: this thinker revived Florine Stettheimer's reputation in the 1980s by praising her Cathedrals of New York series
Art Historian: Kathleen Gilje reimagined Manet's A Bar at the Folies-Bergere with this art historian as the central figure
Art Historian: this thinker wrote an essay on Cezanne titled 'Studies in Contrast' about old peasants with 'huge, sagging bottoms' meant to highlight impotency
Art Historian: this thinker analyzed Berthe Morisot's painting of a wet nurse in terms of the 'Construction of Work and Leisure'
